segments.title: Why carriers were a difficult treaty category
segments.summary: Carrier limits were difficult to design because the ships were still experimental and their military value was unsettled in 1921–22.
segments.body: The opening frames aircraft carriers as a particularly awkward Washington Treaty problem. Early carriers were few, experimental, and often incomplete, so negotiators had little reliable evidence for deciding what counted as a carrier or how much capability a tonnage allowance bought. The discussion also introduces the tension between conversion allowances and purpose-built ships.

evidence.note: The transcript describes early carriers as experimental and explains the conversion problem.

segments.title: Carrier ratios and Japan’s strategic position
segments.summary: The treaty’s carrier allocation could make Japan feel relatively weaker as carrier aviation became more important.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke compares the original 5:5:3 carrier ratio with the positions of France and Italy. He argues that a numerical compromise that seemed tolerable while carriers were unproven could become politically sharper once naval aviation mattered more. The perceived imbalance helps explain why Japanese planners would seek reconnaissance capacity elsewhere, including cruisers.

evidence.note: The ratio comparison links carrier allocations to Japanese reconnaissance and planning choices.

segments.title: The Washington Treaty’s carrier tonnage rules
segments.summary: A close reading of the carrier articles shows how total allowances, the 27,000-ton cap, and conversion exceptions constrained fleet design.
segments.body: This section walks through the treaty’s total carrier allocations and individual displacement limits. It shows why two 33,000-ton conversions could consume so much of a national allowance that little room remained for other carriers. It also distinguishes what the text legally permitted from the conversion-focused expectation negotiators had in mind.

evidence.note: The transcript quotes and interprets Articles 7, 9, and 10.

segments.title: Why purpose-built carriers used tonnage better
segments.summary: Comparing Furious, Eagle, Hermes, and Hōshō shows why purpose-built carriers could carry more aircraft for the same treaty tonnage.
segments.body: The video compares early British and Japanese carriers through tons per aircraft rather than displacement alone. Converted hulls carry the inherited compromises of their original design, while purpose-built ships can devote more of their footprint to hangars and aircraft handling. Carrier size also matters because fixed systems consume a smaller share of a larger ship’s volume.

evidence.note: The comparison gives displacement and aircraft-capacity calculations for early carriers.

segments.title: Carrier aviation before the strike-carrier era
segments.summary: Early carrier value lay in scouting, counter-reconnaissance, spotting, and trade protection as much as in striking enemy ships.
segments.body: The Royal Navy’s interest in carrier numbers is presented as a response to reconnaissance and fleet-protection needs, not simply a search for ship-killing aviation. Aircraft could find enemy forces, disrupt their scouts, and spot fall of shot for battleships beyond direct visual range. This makes carrier capacity part of a wider fleet information system.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses air-group size, trade protection, reconnaissance, and gunnery spotting.

segments.title: A proposed equitable carrier treaty
segments.summary: The alternate treaty adopts a 10:10:7:4:4 ratio, 33,000-ton individual cap, staged conversion rules, and longer service lives.
segments.body: The proposed settlement carries the video’s earlier capital-ship ratio into carrier allocations while avoiding fractional ship allowances. It permits much larger French and Italian carrier totals than the historical treaty, then uses conversion deadlines and service-life rules to pace construction. The point is not that the result is historically inevitable, but that the rules must give every participant a stake in remaining inside them.

evidence.note: The transcript sets the alternative ratio, tonnage totals, conversion timing, and service-life rules.

segments.title: How national carrier requirements shape fleet choices
segments.summary: Different oceans, bases, and fleet missions could lead Britain, the United States, Japan, France, and Italy toward different carrier sizes and numbers.
segments.body: The video treats tonnage as a force-structure choice rather than an automatic instruction to build the largest permitted ship. Italian concentration in the Mediterranean, French commitments across several seas, British three-fleet planning, and American Atlantic–Pacific division produce different incentives. It also stresses that carrier roles in the 1920s centred on reconnaissance, air defence, spotting, and limited anti-submarine work before strike doctrine matured.

evidence.note: The transcript compares national allocations and explains evolving carrier missions.

segments.title: A British 27,500-ton carrier programme
segments.summary: A 27,500-ton British new build is presented as a compromise between carrier numbers, useful air groups, and the protection of later armoured designs.
segments.body: The proposed British programme keeps smaller conversions while using a 27,500-ton new design as a practical middle course. Dr. Clarke argues that this size could support a decent air group and useful protection without committing the fleet entirely to very large ships. He then compares that gradual programme with one based on early G3 conversions and fewer, more powerful strike carriers.

evidence.note: The transcript develops the 27,500-ton new build and contrasts Courageous and G3 conversion paths.

segments.title: What a G3 carrier conversion might enable
segments.summary: A G3 conversion could combine a large flight deck, substantial hangar space, flagship facilities, and the possibility of operating larger aircraft.
segments.body: Using Eagle and Ark Royal as design reference points, the video sketches how a G3 hull might be adapted without relocating its machinery. Its long, broad hull could support a large flight deck, multiple lifts, maintenance space, and a carrier-squadron flagship role. The discussion also considers whether a larger deck might make twin-engine naval aircraft more practical as engines improved.

evidence.note: The transcript examines G3 dimensions, conversion layout, and possible twin-engine operations.

segments.title: Treaty limits, security, and deterrence
segments.summary: The closing argument is that an arms-control system endures only when it provides security and an incentive for every power to keep it.
segments.body: The conclusion connects carrier rules to the larger political purpose of treaty design. It argues that peace cannot rest on freezing construction so tightly that states feel exposed while technology changes around them. A durable settlement instead needs a credible balance: enough preparedness to deter opportunism, but not so much imbalance that fear and resentment encourage a new arms race.

evidence.note: The transcript links treaty compliance, security, continued construction, and deterrence.
